item.text: The baseline is level 1 with no moves and no replay yet. I’m now reading the compact perception helpers and the harness entry points, then I’ll probe each of the five actions on fresh clones and summarize only changed regions/objects.

item.text: The first experiment identifies the avatar unambiguously: the 2×2 color-14 object at pixel `(40,10)` moves by two pixels under actions `1/2/3/4`; action `6` is context-dependent/non-moving. I’m reducing the board to its natural 2×2 tile grid next, then testing `6` beside each reachable special object and tracking object-count/distance changes as the dense signal.

item.text: Reachability is constrained exactly as the board suggests: the avatar can move only on color-2 cells, so it is confined to the lower blue component; backgrounds and the other blue components are unreachable directly. The first `6` shows no pixels, so I’m testing it as a latent “attach/select” toggle followed by movement from each edge of the component—this is the key context the single-action probe could not reveal.

The latent-toggle hypothesis is falsified: `6` without coordinates never changes movement or the board in any tested position. Because this action surface explicitly supports `[6,x,y]`, I’m switching to coordinate probes at the centers of every distinct object class; the earlier no-op was likely an incomplete mouse action, not a game mechanic.

item.text: Coordinate probing found the actual interaction: clicking either right-hand colored glyph changes only its matching left-hand piece and removes that glyph’s outline. I’m now separating “selection” from “motion” by recording the matching piece’s bbox after the click and after each arrow key; I’m also testing a second click to see whether the glyph is a one-shot transform or a control selector.

The mechanics now fit a coherent bridge puzzle. Yellow toggles between the top blue island and a vertical bridge joining the avatar’s lower island to the middle island; green toggles between a horizontal middle bridge and a vertical bridge toward the top. I’ve derived a staged route—cross yellow while down, traverse green/orange, raise green and yellow behind the avatar, then climb to the pink tile—and I’m validating that entire route on one clone before committing it.

The clone validation succeeds: reward rises exactly when the avatar enters the pink tile, after 20 actions, and the next level frame appears. I’m replacing the probes with a parameterized two-stage bridge-traversal leg and making `play_level_1` only compose that leg; then I’ll run the real harness and replay validation.
